Name: latency_aware_spectral_skipping
Title: Latency-Aware Spectral Skipping: PAC-Bayes Bounds for Training-Free Layer Skipping
Short Hypothesis: Layer skipping can be optimized for latency-critical inference by leveraging PAC-Bayes spectral stability bounds to minimize hidden-state perturbation while strictly adhering to wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Existing work on layer skipping (e.g., HiLoRA) focuses on task-specific adaptation or training-based approaches, which are impractical for latency-critical scenarios. Our proposal uniquely integrates PAC-Bayes spectral stability bounds to enforce strict latency guarantees without training, distinguishing it from heuristic-based methods.
Abstract: Layer skipping is a promising technique for reducing inference latency in deep neural networks, but existing methods often rely on heuristic or task-specific adaptations that fail to guarantee strict wall-clock budgets. We propose a training-free framework that leverages PAC-Bayes spectral stability bounds to optimize layer skipping for latency-critical inference. By minimizing hidden-state perturbation while adhering to strict latency constraints, our method ensures both computational efficiency and model accuracy. We introduce a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework that dynamically adjusts skipping thresholds based on spectral stability metrics, providing rigorous guarantees on inference latency. Experiments on vision and language models demonstrate significant reductions in inference time (up to 40%) with minimal accuracy loss (<1%), outperforming existing heuristic approaches. Our work bridges the gap between theoretical guarantees and practical latency-aware inference, offering a principled solution for real-time applications.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate latency reduction and accuracy trade-offs on vision models (ResNet, ViT) using ImageNet. Metrics: Inference time, Top-1 accuracy, Spectral stability radius.
Experiment 2: Assess scalability on language models (BERT, GPT-2) using GLUE benchmarks. Metrics: Latency, Perplexity, Hidden-state perturbation.
Experiment 3: Compare against heuristic layer-skipping methods (e.g., HiLoRA) on domain generalization tasks. Metrics: Inference time, Accuracy, Parameter efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Spectral stability bounds may not generalize across all architectures, limiting applicability.
Risk 2: Dynamic threshold adjustment could introduce computational overhead, offsetting latency gains.
Limitation: The method assumes access to layer-wise Jacobian computations, which may not be feasible in all deployment settings.
